summary: English Romantic poet known for his Great Odes of 1819, 'On First Looking into Chapman's Homer,' and the concept of negative capability. Died of tuberculosis in Rome at age 25.

works.name: "Ode on a Grecian Urn"
works.description: Addresses an 'Attic shape' described as a 'foster-child of Silence and slow Time' and 'unravish'd bride of quietness.' Declares 'Beauty is truth, truth beauty — that is all / Ye know on earth, and all ye need to know.' 'Heard melodies are sweet, but those unheard are sweeter.' 'More happy love! more happy, happy love!' 'What pipes and timbrels? What wild ecstasy?' An ekphrastic poem. Referenced in Pale Fire as 'Red Sox Win 5-4 on Chapman's Homer.' Tony Harrison's 'A Kumquat for John Keats' references it.

clues.clue: 'Beauty is truth, truth beauty' — addressing an 'Attic shape,' 'foster-child of Silence and slow Time'
examples: declared 'Beauty is truth, truth beauty' in 'Ode on a Grecian Urn'
calls an object a 'foster-child of silence and slow time'

clues.clue: 'Heard melodies are sweet, but those unheard are sweeter'
examples: wrote that 'heard melodies are sweet, but those unheard are sweeter'

clues.clue: 'For ever warm and still to be enjoy'd, / For ever panting, and for ever young'
examples: cries, 'For ever warm and still to be enjoy'd, / For ever panting, and for ever young'

works.name: "Ode to a Nightingale"
works.description: Speaker desires to 'die upon the midnight with no pain.' Asks 'Do I wake or sleep?' after declaring 'thou wast not born for death, immortal Bird!' The word 'forlorn' tolls the speaker 'back from thee to my sole self.' 'Fled is that music.' Flying 'on the viewless wings of poesy.' Earl Wasserman critiqued it using Keats's 'Mansion of Many Apartments' metaphor. Melody transforms into a 'plaintive anthem.' 'Where youth grows pale, and spectre-thin, and dies.'

clues.clue: 'Do I wake or sleep?'; 'thou wast not born for death, immortal Bird!'; 'forlorn' tolls him back
examples: asks 'do I wake or sleep' after declaring 'thou wast not born for death, immortal Bird!'
the word 'forlorn' prompts the speaker to claim 'the very word is like a bell to toll me back'

clues.clue: Desire to 'die upon the midnight with no pain'
examples: talks about a desire to die 'upon the midnight with no pain'

clues.clue: Opens: 'My heart aches, and a drowsy numbness pains / My sense, as though hemlock had I drunk'
examples: opened another poem by stating 'My heart aches, and a drowsy numbness pains / My sense'

works.name: "On First Looking into Chapman's Homer"
works.description: Opens 'Much have I travell'd in the realms of gold.' The speaker feels 'like some watcher of the skies when a new planet swims into his ken' or 'like stout Cortez when with eagle eyes he star'd at the Pacific.' A 'deep-brow'd' Homer ruled 'one wide expanse as his demesne.' Referenced in Pale Fire as 'Red Sox Win 5-4 on Chapman's Homer.'

clues.clue: 'Much have I travell'd in the realms of gold'; feels like 'stout Cortez' staring at the Pacific
examples: 'Much have I travell'd in the realms of gold' opens this author's poem
felt like 'stout Cortez when with eagle eyes he star'd at the Pacific'

clues.clue: Feels 'like some watcher of the skies when a new planet swims into his ken'
examples: felt like he was 'some watcher of the skies when a new planet swims into his ken'

works.name: "When I Have Fears That I May Cease to Be"
works.description: Anticipates Keats's death from tuberculosis. Ends 'On the shore / of the wide world I stand alone, and think / till love and fame to nothingness do sink.' Imagines tracing 'huge cloudy symbols of a high romance.' 'The faery power of unreflecting love.'

clues.clue: Ends 'love and fame to nothingness do sink'; anticipates death; 'huge cloudy symbols of a high romance'
examples: 'On the shore / of the wide world I stand alone'
laments 'that I may never live to trace / Their shadows with the magic hand of chance'

works.name: "La Belle Dame Sans Merci"
works.description: A ballad beginning 'O what can ail thee, knight-at-arms / Alone and palely loitering?' A haggard knight by a lake 'where the sedge has wither'd and no birds sing' recounts a 'fairy's child' who gives him 'roots of relish sweet, / And honey wild, and manna-dew.' Kings, princes, and warriors appear 'death-pale.' Frank Dicksee and John William Waterhouse painted scenes from it.

clues.clue: 'Alone and palely loitering' by a lake; 'the sedge has wither'd and no birds sing'; fairy's child
examples: opens with a 'haggard' knight who is 'alone and palely loitering' by a lake where 'the sedge has wither'd'

clues.clue: Dicksee painting: woman on horse looking down at a man; Waterhouse: maroon-clad woman wrapping scarf
examples: A work by this author inspired a Frank Dicksee painting in which a woman on a horse looks down at a man

works.name: "The Eve of St. Agnes"
works.description: Madeline falls asleep on the title night to see her future husband in a dream. Porphyro lays out 'candied apple, quince and plum and gourd' as she sleeps. Old nurse Angela and the Beadsman die the same night Porphyro and Madeline elope in a storm. Begins and ends with references to the Beadsman. Porphyro picks up a lute and recites Alain Chartier's La Belle Dame sans Mercy. Millais and Holman Hunt painted scenes.

clues.clue: Madeline sleeps to see future husband; Porphyro lays out fruits; elope in storm; Angela and Beadsman die
examples: The old nurse Angela and the Beadsman die on the same night that Porphyro and Madeline elope
'supperless to bed she must retire' in order that she 'might have visions of delight'

works.name: "To Autumn"
works.description: 'Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness.' Sun conspires with Autumn to 'bless with fruit the vines.' 'Where are the songs of spring? Ay, where are they?' — ubi sunt formula. Ends with swallows 'twitter[ing] in the skies.' Keats abandoned Hyperion and wrote 'To Autumn' on the same day.

clues.clue: 'Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness'; 'Where are the songs of spring?'; swallows twittering
examples: calls Autumn a 'Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness'
'Where are the songs of spring? Ay, Where are they?'

works.name: Hyperion and Endymion
works.description: Hyperion is an unfinished epic about the fall of the Titans, abandoned because of 'too many Miltonic inversions.' Oceanus cites 'eternal law' that 'first in beauty should be first in might.' Hyperion asks 'Saturn is fallen, am I too to fall?' Endymion opens 'A thing of beauty is a joy forever' and includes a 'Hymn to Pan' that Wordsworth called 'a very pretty piece of paganism.' Cynthia is Endymion's lover.

clues.clue: Hyperion: unfinished epic of Titans; abandoned for 'too many Miltonic inversions'
examples: abandoned that poem because 'there were too many Miltonic inversions in it'
an unfinished epic about the fall of the Titan Hyperion

clues.clue: Endymion: 'A thing of beauty is a joy forever'; Hymn to Pan; Wordsworth's 'pretty piece of paganism'
examples: 'A thing of beauty is a joy forever' begins Endymion
a 'Hymn to Pan' that led Wordsworth to quip 'a very pretty piece of paganism'

works.name: Letters and Negative Capability
works.description: Keats's letters, which T. S. Eliot called 'the most notable ever written by any English poet,' contain the concept of negative capability: 'being in uncertainties, Mysteries, doubts, without any irritable reaching after fact and reason.' Also contain: the 'Mansion of Many Apartments' metaphor (life as rooms), the 'vale of soul-making' (borrowed by John Hick for his theodicy), the 'chameleon Poet' (no self), 'the sense of Beauty overcomes every other consideration.' He annotated Paradise Lost and wrote 'Lines on Seeing a Lock of Milton's Hair.' At Benjamin Haydon's 'immortal dinner,' Keats said Newton 'destroyed all the poetry of the rainbow' (inspiring Dawkins's Unweaving the Rainbow). Walter Jackson Bate wrote the premier biography. Keats names a heuristic for the rhyme-as-reason effect. Countee Cullen dedicated a poem to him 'At Spring Time.'

clues.clue: Negative capability: 'being in uncertainties, Mysteries, doubts, without any irritable reaching after fact and reason'
examples: praised the ability of 'being in uncertainties, mysteries, doubts, without any irritable reaching after fact and reason'

clues.clue: 'Mansion of Many Apartments' metaphor for life
examples: compares 'human life to a large Mansion of Many Apartments'

clues.clue: Newton 'destroyed all the poetry of the rainbow' — Haydon's 'immortal dinner'
examples: remarked that Newton had 'destroyed all the poetry of the rainbow'

clues.clue: Died of tuberculosis in Rome; epitaph 'Here lies One whose Name was writ in Water'; subject of Shelley's Adonais
examples: this event in which the author of 'On First Looking into Chapman's Homer' succumbed to tuberculosis
this man's soul 'beacons from the abode where the Eternal are'

works.name: Other Poems
works.description: Isabella; or the Pot of Basil: brothers discover Lorenzo's head in a pot (from the Decameron). 'Ode to Psyche': 'Yes I will be thy priest'; 'pale-mouth'd prophet dreaming.' 'Ode on Melancholy': 'No, no, go not to Lethe'; 'dwells with Beauty — Beauty that must die.' 'Ode on Indolence': three figures in white robes; 'Vanish, ye phantoms!' 'On the Grasshopper and the Cricket': 'the Poetry of earth is never dead.' 'Bright Star': 'nature's patient, sleepless Eremite'; titles a Jane Campion biopic. 'Sleep and Poetry': 'the soft closer of our eyes.' Inspired Pre-Raphaelites (Millais, Holman Hunt).

clues.clue: Isabella: brothers find Lorenzo's head in a pot of basil; from Decameron
examples: two horrified brothers discover Lorenzo's head in the title container

clues.clue: 'Bright Star': 'nature's patient, sleepless Eremite'; Jane Campion biopic

comprehensive_summary:
John Keats is one of the most frequently clued British Romantic poets. His 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' is the primary giveaway, declaring 'Beauty is truth, truth beauty' about an 'Attic shape' described as a 'foster-child of Silence and slow Time.' 'Ode to a Nightingale' features the desire to 'die upon the midnight with no pain' and asks 'Do I wake or sleep?' 'On First Looking into Chapman's Homer' opens 'Much have I travell'd in the realms of gold' and compares the experience to 'stout Cortez' staring at the Pacific.

Other major poems include 'When I Have Fears' (anticipating death), 'La Belle Dame Sans Merci' (a knight 'alone and palely loitering'), 'The Eve of St. Agnes' (Porphyro and Madeline), 'To Autumn' ('Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness'), and the unfinished Hyperion (abandoned for 'too many Miltonic inversions'). Endymion opens 'A thing of beauty is a joy forever.'

Keats's letters, called by Eliot 'the most notable ever written by any English poet,' contain the concept of negative capability ('being in uncertainties without irritable reaching after fact and reason') and the 'Mansion of Many Apartments' metaphor. He died of tuberculosis in Rome at 25; his epitaph reads 'Here lies One whose Name was writ in Water.' Shelley's elegy Adonais commemorates his death, with his soul 'beaconing from the abode where the Eternal are.' Byron dismissed his death as being 'snuffed out by an article.'
